walkthrough.title: Place the Round Track
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Sagrada. First, find the Round Track and place it near the center of the table. This double-sided piece has a Round Track on one side for tracking the ten rounds of play, and a Score Track on the back for final scoring. Start with the Round Track side face up.

walkthrough.title: Reveal Tool Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all twelve Tool Cards and reveal three of them face up near the Round Track. These three tools will be available to all players throughout the game. Each tool lets you bend the normal placement rules in a specific way, but they cost Favor Tokens to use. Set the remaining tool cards aside.

walkthrough.title: Reveal Public Objectives
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all ten Public Objective Cards and reveal three face up near the tools. These three objectives are the shared scoring goals that all players will try to fulfill. Public objectives reward patterns like sets of different colors, specific value combinations, or complete rows and columns. Set the remaining objective cards aside.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Dice Bag
walkthrough.text: Find all ninety dice, eighteen each in five colors: red, yellow, green, blue, and purple. Place every single die into the cloth dice bag and give it a good mix. These dice represent the glass pieces you will be placing in your stained glass window throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Deal Private Objectives
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the five Private Objective Cards and deal one face down to each player. Look at your card but keep it secret. Your private objective tells you which color of dice scores bonus points for you at the end of the game. You will score the sum of all pip values on dice of your private color in your finished window.

walkthrough.title: Deal Window Pattern Cards
walkthrough.text: Deal two random Window Pattern Cards to each player. Each card has a different pattern on each side, giving you four options to choose from. The patterns show colored spaces, numbered spaces, and blank white spaces. Colored spaces require a die of that exact color. Numbered spaces require a die of that exact value.

walkthrough.title: Give Out Window Frames
walkthrough.text: Give each player one Window Frame Player Board. This is the frame that holds your chosen window pattern. The frame has a groove at the bottom where you slide your chosen pattern card into place. Each player's window is a four-by-five grid of spaces where dice will be placed.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Window Pattern
walkthrough.text: Now each player chooses one of their four pattern options. Look at the difficulty number in the bottom corner of each pattern, ranging from three to six. Higher difficulty gives you more Favor Tokens but also has more restrictions. Consider your private objective color when choosing. A pattern with many spaces matching your private color can score well.

walkthrough.title: Collect Favor Tokens
walkthrough.text: After choosing your pattern, take Favor Tokens from the supply equal to your pattern's difficulty number. A difficulty three pattern gives you three tokens, a difficulty six pattern gives you six. Favor Tokens are your currency for using Tool Cards. Each unspent token is also worth one VP at the end of the game, so spend them wisely.

walkthrough.title: Place Score Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a Score Marker in their color and places it near the Round Track. You will use these on the Score Track side during final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Choose the Starting Player
walkthrough.text: Randomly choose who goes first. The rulebook suggests the most recent cathedral visitor. Give the dice bag to the starting player. The bag passes clockwise each round, and whoever holds the bag is that round's start player. You are ready to begin. Let's learn the rules.

Score Track on reverse for endgame scoring

Scores sum of pip values for dice of your private color

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Sagrada is played over exactly ten rounds. Each round, dice are drawn from the bag, rolled, and placed into a shared Draft Pool. Players take turns drafting one die at a time and placing it into their window grid. The goal is to fill your stained glass window while scoring as many points as possible from public objectives, your private objective, and leftover Favor Tokens.

walkthrough.title: The Draft Pool
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, the start player draws dice from the bag and rolls them. The number of dice depends on player count: five dice for two players, seven for three, nine for four. The formula is two dice per player plus one extra. These rolled dice form the Draft Pool for the round, and everyone will be drafting from this shared pool.

walkthrough.title: The Snake Draft
walkthrough.text: Turns follow a snake draft pattern each round. First, each player takes one turn in clockwise order. Then the order reverses, and each player takes a second turn in counterclockwise order. This means the last player in clockwise order gets two consecutive turns. The snake draft helps balance the advantage of going first versus going last.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Two Actions
walkthrough.text: First, you may draft one die from the Pool and place it in your window. Second, you may use one Tool Card by spending Favor Tokens. You can do both, just one, or neither. You never have to draft a die if no legal placement exists or if you choose to pass.

walkthrough.title: Die Placement: First Die
walkthrough.text: You cannot start in the middle. This means your first placement options are limited to the sixteen spaces along the border of your four-by-five grid.

walkthrough.title: Die Placement: Adjacency Rule
walkthrough.text: After your first die, every subsequent die must be placed adjacent to an existing die in your window. Adjacent means touching orthogonally or diagonally, so each die has up to eight possible neighbor positions. You cannot place a die in an isolated spot with no neighbors.

walkthrough.title: Die Placement: Restriction Matching
walkthrough.text: Your window pattern has three types of spaces. Colored spaces require a die of that exact color. Numbered spaces require a die of that exact value. White spaces accept any die as long as all other rules are followed. Always check the pattern restriction before placing.

walkthrough.title: Die Placement: Orthogonal Conflicts
walkthrough.text: This is the trickiest rule. No two orthogonally adjacent dice may share the same color. No two orthogonally adjacent dice may share the same value. Diagonal neighbors can match freely. This means every die you place must differ from its up, down, left, and right neighbors in both color and value. This constraint gets increasingly difficult as your window fills up.

walkthrough.title: Using Tool Cards
walkthrough.text: Tool Cards let you bend the normal rules. Each tool has a specific effect, like moving a die you already placed, changing a die's value, or ignoring a placement restriction for one placement. To use a tool, spend Favor Tokens and place them on the Tool Card. The first player to use a particular tool pays one token. Every use after that costs two tokens.

walkthrough.title: End of Round Cleanup
walkthrough.text: After all players have taken both turns, any dice remaining in the Draft Pool are placed on the current round space of the Round Track. These leftover dice accumulate on the track throughout the game. Then pass the dice bag clockwise. The player who receives the bag is the start player for the next round.

walkthrough.title: Public Objective Scoring
walkthrough.text: At game end, each of the three Public Objective Cards scores for all players. Public objectives reward patterns in your window. Some reward complete rows or columns with no repeated colors or values. Some reward sets of dice meeting specific criteria. You can score each public objective multiple times if you meet the condition multiple times.

walkthrough.title: Private Objective Scoring
walkthrough.text: Your Private Objective scores the sum of pip values on all dice of your secret color in your window. If your private color is green and you have four green dice showing values two, three, five, and six, your private objective scores sixteen points. This makes your private color extremely valuable to draft.

walkthrough.title: Favor Token Scoring and Penalties
walkthrough.text: Each unspent Favor Token is worth one VP at game end. Every empty space in your window costs you one VP. Filling your window completely avoids penalties and maximizes scoring opportunities. Balance spending tokens on tools versus keeping them for their point value.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring and Winning
walkthrough.text: Flip the Round Track to its Score Track side. Add up VP from three Public Objectives, your Private Objective, and unspent Favor Tokens. Subtract one VP per empty space. The highest total wins. Tiebreakers go to most Private Objective points, then most remaining Favor Tokens, then reverse player order in the final round.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: 10 rounds of dice drafting and window building
Draft dice from shared pool → place in your grid
Score from public objectives + private objective + unspent tokens - empty spaces

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Cathedral
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three players. Each player has a different window pattern and private objective. We will practice drafting dice, placing them legally, and see some common mistakes. Let's build some stained glass.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Setup
walkthrough.text: Player one has a difficulty four pattern with mostly color restrictions. Player two chose a difficulty five pattern with many number restrictions. Player three picked a difficulty three pattern that is mostly open white spaces. Each player has received Favor Tokens matching their difficulty. The three public objectives reward sets of different colors in a row, pairs of specific values, and complete columns.

walkthrough.title: Rolling the Draft Pool
walkthrough.text: With three players, we draw seven dice from the bag. The start player reaches in, pulls out seven random dice, and rolls them all. We get a red three, blue five, green two, yellow four, purple one, green six, and blue three. These seven dice form our Draft Pool for the round. Let's start drafting.

walkthrough.title: Player One: First Placement
walkthrough.text: Player one looks at their window pattern. They find a blue restriction space on the top-left corner. The blue five from the pool fits perfectly. They draft the blue five and place it on that corner space. Since this is their first die, it must go on an edge or corner, and this corner space has a blue restriction. The color matches. A clean, legal placement.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: First Placement
walkthrough.text: Player two has a number four restriction on the right edge. The yellow four is a perfect match. They draft it and place it on that edge space. Player two also noted that yellow is their private objective color, so this die will score four points toward their private objective at game end. Smart drafting considers both placement needs and private scoring.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Ignoring Edge Rule
walkthrough.text: Player three looks at the green two and wants to place it in the center of their window on a number two restriction space. But wait. This is their first die, and it must go on an edge or corner. The center is illegal for a first placement. Player three has to reconsider and find an edge or corner space that works.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: First Die Must Be on the Border
walkthrough.text: Your very first die of the entire game must go on one of the sixteen edge or corner spaces of your grid. This is easy to forget because the center often has tempting restriction matches. Plan your first placement carefully. Ideally, start on an edge that lets you build toward the center over the next few rounds.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Corrects and Places
walkthrough.text: Player three finds the green two can go on a bottom-left corner space that has no restriction, which is a white space. It is on the edge, so the first die rule is satisfied. The green two is placed legally. Now the snake draft reverses direction.

walkthrough.title: Second Turn: Snake Draft Reversal
walkthrough.text: Now the order reverses. Player three gets their second turn first, then Player two, then Player one. Player three drafts the purple one and places it adjacent to their green two. The colors differ and the values differ, so the orthogonal conflict rule is satisfied.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's Mistake: Orthogonal Color Conflict
walkthrough.text: Player two wants to draft the blue three and place it directly to the left of their yellow four. But they already have plans for a yellow die next round in the space above the blue three's target spot. They place the blue three. Then on a future turn, when they try to place a blue die directly above this blue three, they realize blue cannot be orthogonally adjacent to blue. They have blocked themselves.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Think Two Moves Ahead
walkthrough.text: The orthogonal conflict rule means every placement affects future options. Before placing a die, look at the empty spaces around it. What colors and values might you need there later? If you place a blue three next to a space that requires blue, you have created a conflict. Always scan the nearby restrictions and plan at least two placements ahead.

walkthrough.title: Player One Drafts Strategically
walkthrough.text: Their private objective is green, so this die scores six points toward their private objective. They place it on a white space adjacent to their blue five. Blue and green differ in color, five and six differ in value. Perfectly legal. Player one is building their window while maximizing private objective scoring.

walkthrough.title: Leftover Dice Go to the Round Track
walkthrough.text: After all players have taken both turns, one die remains in the Draft Pool. The red three goes onto the round one space of the Round Track. These leftover dice accumulate throughout the game. The dice bag passes clockwise, and the new holder becomes start player for round two.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's Mistake: Wasting Favor Tokens
walkthrough.text: In round two, Player two spends two Favor Tokens on a Tool Card to slightly adjust a die value from three to four. The benefit is marginal because they could have found a legal placement without the tool. Two tokens spent for very little gain. Those tokens would have been worth two VP at game end, which is a high price for a minor convenience.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Spend Tokens Only for High-Impact Tools
walkthrough.text: Favor Tokens are both a tool currency and victory points. Every token spent is a point lost. Only use tools when they solve a real problem: fixing a placement that would otherwise create a dead space, enabling a high-value die placement, or preventing an empty space penalty. Do not use tools for marginal improvements that you could work around with different drafting.

walkthrough.title: How the Game Develops
walkthrough.text: As rounds progress, your window fills up and the orthogonal conflict rule becomes increasingly challenging. By round seven or eight, you may find that only certain dice from the Draft Pool can legally fit in your remaining spaces. This is why early placement planning matters so much. The players who think ahead have more options in the late game.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues for all ten rounds. Each round adds more dice to your window and more leftover dice to the Round Track. Keep your Public Objectives and Private Objective in mind with every draft decision. The player who balances all scoring sources while minimizing empty spaces will come out on top.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have learned the draft and placement rules, seen the snake draft in action, and encountered three common mistakes. Remember: first die goes on the edge, check orthogonal neighbors before placing, and spend Favor Tokens only when the impact is worth the point you are giving up.

walkthrough.title: Before You Build
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on drafting and window planning.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to placement rules and tool effects.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun building!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the biggest beginner traps in Sagrada. First, placing your first die in a corner that blocks future expansion. Start somewhere that keeps multiple paths open. Second, ignoring orthogonal conflicts until you get stuck in the late game. Always check the four cardinal neighbors before placing. Third, burning Favor Tokens on low-impact tool uses. Each token is worth a point, so the tool benefit must exceed one VP to be worthwhile.

walkthrough.title: Window Pattern Selection
walkthrough.text: When choosing your window pattern, consider three things. First, does the pattern have spaces that match your private objective color? More matching spaces mean more opportunities to score your private color. Second, how restrictive is the pattern? Patterns with many colored and numbered spaces are harder to fill but give more Favor Tokens. Third, does the pattern naturally support the public objectives? If a public objective rewards all different colors in a row, a pattern with color variety built in gives you a head start.

walkthrough.title: Draft for Your Private Objective
walkthrough.text: Your private objective is a massive scoring source. If your private color is blue, every blue die you place scores its full pip value. A blue six is worth six private points on its own. When choosing between two equally legal placements, always lean toward your private color. High-value dice of your private color are your most valuable drafts.

walkthrough.title: Build From the Edges In
walkthrough.text: Since your first die must go on the edge, plan your expansion route inward. The most flexible approach is to start from a corner and build in an L-shape, giving yourself multiple adjacency options for future dice. Avoid building in a straight line along one edge because that limits your next placements to a narrow path. Keep at least two or three open adjacency points at all times.

walkthrough.title: Identify Hard Spaces Early
walkthrough.text: Look at your window and identify the two or three hardest spaces to fill. These are spaces with tight restrictions surrounded by other restricted spaces. Plan your placement route to reach these hard spaces while you still have draft flexibility. If you leave the hardest spaces for the last few rounds, you may find that no available dice can legally fill them.

walkthrough.title: Public Objective Awareness
walkthrough.text: The three public objectives are shared by everyone, so they represent the highest-value scoring opportunities. Study them before your first draft. If one objective rewards all five different colors in a row, start thinking about which row you will dedicate to that pattern. Public objectives that can be scored multiple times are especially valuable because they reward concentrated effort.

walkthrough.title: Tool Card Timing
walkthrough.text: Tools become more valuable as your window fills up. In the early game, you usually have plenty of legal placements. In the late game, a tool that moves a die or changes a value can save you from an empty space penalty. Consider saving your Favor Tokens for rounds seven through ten when tools provide the biggest rescue value. The exception is a tool that fundamentally restructures your window early for a massive scoring advantage.

walkthrough.title: Snake Draft Positioning
walkthrough.text: The snake draft means your position in the turn order shifts every round. When you are first to draft, pick the die that best advances your private objective or fills a critical restriction. When you are last before the reversal, you get two consecutive picks, which lets you plan a mini-combo. Think about both your picks during the reversal window and draft the more contested die first.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't block expansion with your first placement
Don't ignore orthogonal conflicts until it's too late
Don't burn Favor Tokens on low-impact tool uses

Always lean toward private color when equally legal

Avoid straight-line edge builds (limits future paths)
Keep 2-3 open adjacency points at all times

summary.title: Identify Hard Spaces Early

Plan route to reach them while draft is flexible
Leaving hard spaces for last = likely empty space penalties

summary.title: Public Objective Awareness
bullets: Study all 3 public objectives before first draft
Plan which rows/columns to dedicate to each pattern
Multi-score objectives = highest value

summary.title: Tool Card Timing
bullets: Early game: usually plenty of legal placements (save tokens)
Rounds 7-10: tools rescue from empty space penalties
Exception: early structural tool use for massive scoring advantage

summary.title: Snake Draft Positioning
bullets: First pick: best private objective or critical restriction die
Last before reversal: 2 consecutive picks enable combos
Draft contested die first during reversal window

walkthrough.title: Private Objective Maximization
walkthrough.text: Your private objective can easily score twenty or more points if you prioritize it. Count the spaces in your window where your private color could legally go. Aim to place five or six dice of your private color with high pip values. A window with five blue dice averaging four pips each scores twenty points from the private objective alone. Draft aggressively for your color, especially high-value dice.

walkthrough.title: Public Objective Stacking
walkthrough.text: The strongest scoring comes from fulfilling public objectives multiple times. If the objective is 'all five different colors in a row,' completing it in two separate rows doubles the score. Plan your window layout to overlap multiple objectives. A row that scores for both a color variety objective and a value variety objective is twice as valuable as one that only hits a single objective.

walkthrough.title: Constraint Mapping
walkthrough.text: Before your first draft, map out your entire window mentally. Identify which spaces are tightly constrained by color or value requirements. Then identify which spaces are white and flexible. Your strategy should route through constrained spaces first when the Draft Pool offers matching dice, and use flexible spaces as buffers that can absorb whatever is left. Constrained spaces dictate your path through the grid.

walkthrough.title: Denial Drafting
walkthrough.text: In a competitive game, watch what your opponents need. If Player two desperately needs a yellow die to complete a row and there is only one yellow in the pool, drafting it denies them significant points. Denial is most valuable when the VP impact on your opponent exceeds the VP cost to you of taking a suboptimal die. This calculus becomes more important in two-player games where you directly compete for dice.

walkthrough.title: Empty Space Avoidance
walkthrough.text: Each empty space costs one VP, but more importantly, empty spaces often represent missed opportunities for public and private objective scoring. A single empty space might cost you one penalty VP plus five or more opportunity VP from an incomplete row or missing private color die. Preventing empty spaces should be a top priority from round six onward. Use tools specifically to avoid dead spaces.

walkthrough.title: Tool Card Mastery
walkthrough.text: Each Tool Card fundamentally changes how you play. A tool that lets you move a placed die opens up restructuring plays that would otherwise be impossible. A tool that ignores color restrictions for one placement lets you break through constraint bottlenecks. A tool that lets you flip a die to its opposite value turns a one into a six. Learn all twelve tools so you can instantly recognize when one creates a high-value play.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Draft Pool Reading
walkthrough.text: In rounds eight through ten, your remaining open spaces have very specific requirements. Before the dice are rolled, know exactly which colors and values can legally fill each space. When the Draft Pool is revealed, immediately identify which dice fit your needs and which are contested. Having a pre-planned priority list for each draft lets you make fast, optimal decisions under pressure.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Optimization Math
walkthrough.text: In the final scoring calculation, the most common trap is focusing on one source at the expense of others. A player who maxes out their private objective at twenty-five points but scores zero from public objectives and has four empty spaces might lose to a balanced player with fifteen private, twelve public, four tokens, and zero empty spaces. Total every source as you play and adjust your strategy to maximize the combined total, not just one component.

summary.title: Private Objective Maximization
bullets: Aim for 5-6 dice of private color with high pip values
5 dice × avg 4 pips = 20 private points
Draft aggressively for private color, especially 5s and 6s

summary.title: Public Objective Stacking
bullets: Fulfill each public objective multiple times for double/triple scoring
Plan rows/columns that overlap 2+ objectives
Multi-objective rows = highest value placements

summary.title: Constraint Mapping
bullets: Map constrained spaces vs flexible white spaces before first draft
Route through constrained spaces first when matching dice available
White spaces = buffers for whatever remains

summary.title: Denial Drafting
bullets: Watch opponents' needs — deny key dice when impact is high
Denial value must exceed cost of suboptimal self-placement
Most impactful in 2-player games

summary.title: Empty Space Avoidance
bullets: Empty space = -1 VP penalty + missed objective VP
1 empty space can cost 5+ total VP (penalty + opportunity)
Priority from round 6 onward — use tools to prevent dead spaces

summary.title: Tool Card Mastery
bullets: Move die = restructuring, ignore color = break bottleneck
Flip die (1↔6, 2↔5, 3↔4) = value manipulation
Learn all 12 tools to recognize high-value plays instantly

summary.title: Late-Game Draft Pool Reading
bullets: Rounds 8-10: know exact color/value needs per open space
Pre-plan priority list before dice are rolled
Fast optimal decisions under pressure

summary.title: Scoring Optimization Math
bullets: Don't tunnel on 1 scoring source — maximize combined total
Balanced 15 private + 12 public + 4 tokens + 0 empty often beats
25 private + 0 public + 0 tokens + 4 empty
Track all sources as you play, adjust accordingly

entries.definition: A card slid into the bottom of your frame board showing a four-by-five grid of placement restrictions. Spaces can be colored (require matching die color), numbered (require matching die value), or white (accept any die).
The player board that holds your window pattern card. Each player has one frame with a groove at the bottom for the pattern card. The frame displays your four-by-five placement grid.
The cloth bag containing all ninety dice. The start player draws dice from the bag each round. The bag passes clockwise after each round.
The set of dice drawn and rolled each round. Two per player plus one extra. All players draft from this shared pool during the round.
A track with ten spaces where leftover dice are placed at the end of each round. Flips to the Score Track for final scoring.
The reverse side of the Round Track used during final scoring to tally each player's VP total.
Tokens received during setup equal to your window's difficulty number. Spent to use Tool Cards. Each unspent token is worth one VP at game end. Twenty-four total in the supply.
Colored markers for tracking each player's final score on the Score Track. Flip markers if score exceeds fifty.
Twelve possible tools, three revealed each game. Each bends a specific placement or die-manipulation rule. First use costs one Favor Token, subsequent uses cost two.
Ten possible objectives, three revealed each game. Shared scoring goals rewarding specific patterns in your window. Can be scored multiple times if conditions are met repeatedly.
Five cards, one per color. Each player receives one secretly. Scores the sum of pip values on all dice of your private color in your finished window.
Your very first die of the game must be placed on an edge or corner space of your window. Sixteen legal starting positions on the four-by-five grid.
After your first die, every subsequent die must be placed touching an existing die orthogonally or diagonally. No isolated placements allowed.
A colored space on your window pattern requires a die of that exact color. Five possible colors: red, yellow, green, blue, purple.
A numbered space on your window pattern requires a die showing that exact pip value. Values range from one to six.
An unrestricted space on your window pattern that accepts any die color and value, as long as orthogonal conflict rules are obeyed.
No two dice that are directly adjacent up, down, left, or right may share the same color. Diagonal neighbors are exempt from this rule.
No two dice that are directly adjacent up, down, left, or right may share the same pip value. Diagonal neighbors are exempt from this rule.
The turn order pattern each round. First pass goes clockwise, second pass reverses counterclockwise. The last player in clockwise order gets two consecutive picks.
First use of a specific Tool Card costs one Favor Token. Every subsequent use of that same card costs two tokens. Tokens are placed on the card when spent.
A number from three to six on each window pattern. Determines how many Favor Tokens you receive. Higher difficulty means more tokens but more restrictive spaces in the pattern.
At game end, each empty (unfilled) space in your window costs one VP. Filling your entire window avoids all penalties.
Sum the pip values of all dice matching your private color in your finished window. A major scoring source that can reach twenty or more points.
Score each of the three shared public objectives. Many can be scored multiple times. Row and column objectives only count fully completed rows or columns.
Each unspent Favor Token is worth one VP at game end. Balancing token spending on tools versus keeping them for VP is a key strategic tension.
If tied on total VP: first tiebreak is most Private Objective points. Second is most remaining Favor Tokens. Third is reverse player order in the final round.
If your window is found to break placement rules, remove dice of your choice until the window is legal. Removed dice are out of play and resulting empty spaces count as penalties.
A strategy technique where you mentally map which spaces are tightly constrained versus flexible before your first draft. Route through constrained spaces first to avoid dead ends.
